english: with pendent silk strips Dzl., Gyatch., Glr. - 2. also śes-rab-ral-gri (shes rab ral gri), stated to be a kind of dagger, set upright, a semblance of which often attends apparitions of the gods; thus the signification of 'sword', given by Sch., seems to be justified, and also Schr. refers to it under spa-dám (spa dam); I never met with it in B. in that sense.
Pers., Urd. بادام, from the Ssk. वाताम, 'windmango' Shksp., almond.

a little hair, the little hairs of the body, bá-spu laṅ (ba spu lang) or ldaṅ (ldang), the little hairs stand up, I shudder, B., C.; similarly: bá-spu γyo (ba spu g.yo) Glr., Mil.; ba-spu tsam yaṅ med (ba spu tsam yang med) (I feel no repentance) even as great as a hair Dzl.; bá-spu-čan (ba spu can) hairy, covered with hair, ba-spu-méd (ba spu med) bald; bá-spui bú-ga (ba spui bu ga) or kuṅ (kung) (-bu) pores.
(Pers. پاپوش pápóš) a soft shoe, skúd-pai (skud pai) knitted shoe, piṅ-pai (ping pai) felt-shoe, but in general they are made of wool or goat's hair.
Mil., Wdn., Cs. and Sch.: 'a species of wild cattle with large horns'; Sch. also: buffalo-calf; though in Sambh. gaṅs-ri-ba-mén (gangs ri ba men) are spoken of.

hoar-frost, B. and col.; ba-tsa (ba tsa) (Campbell in Summer's Phenix p. 142, 5: pen-cha), inferior, impure soda, incrusting the ground near salt-lakes; it is mixed with the food of cattle (from which circumstance the word may be translated 'cow-salt'), occasionally also for the want of something better put into the tea; bá-tsai skyúr-rtsi (ba tsai skyur rtsi) Cs. muriatic (hydro-chloric) acid.
čaṅ-śés bá-la-ha (cang shes ba la ha), n. of a demon, v. rta-mčóg (rta mchog).
= da-lí (da li), various low alpine species of Rhododendron.
medicinal plant, belonging to the climbers Med.
1. v. ba (ba) I. - 2. prob. = bá-śa-ha (ba sha ha) a bitter-tasted officinal plant, acc. to Wdn. an Indian tree; in Lh. a rather insignificant radiated flower.
Ld., ba-śo-ka (ba sho ka) C., currants, small raisins.
elephant's tooth, ivory; bá-so-mk'an (ba so mkhan) worker in ivory.

I. a primary signification of this word seems to be: a narrow space; thus with Sch. fig. bág-dog-pa (bag dog pa) to be straitened, in necessitous circumstances, poor; in another application more frq.: bág-tsam (bag tsam) a little, nor bág-tsam re (nor bag tsam re) a little money Mil.; bag-ré (bag re) Thgr., perh. the same; dáṅ-ga bág-tsam bde (dang ga bag tsam bde) the appetite is growing a little better Lt.; tsér-ma bág-tsam yód-pa (tser ma bag tsam yod pa) having a few prickles Wdn.; bág-tsam-pa (bag tsam pa) slight, insignificant, trifling, sdug-bsṅál (sdug bsngal), a slight misfortune Thgy.; ma-bdé-ba bág-tsam-la bzód-pa mi byéd-pa (ma bde ba bag tsam la bzod pa mi byed pa) Mil. to be fretting on account of a trifling mischance; most frq., however, the word has a moral bearing: attention, care, caution, relative to physical and moral evils or contaminations; bag-méd (bag med), in a gen. sense: rá-ro dáṅ-po bág-med-pa (ra ro dang po bag med pa) the beginning of intoxication is the disappearing of attention; in a special sense (Ssk. प्रमाद): careless, heedless, fearless; mi-bdé-bai (mi bde bai) fearless of misfortune Dzl.; °dí-lta-bui bag méd-par γyur ('di lta bui bag med par gyur) I shall be freed from the fear of such things Dzl.; fearless, without fear or consideration, without regard to consequences or to the judgment of others etc., čaṅ-la bag-méd °dí-tsam °t'uṅ-ba (chang la bag med 'di tsam 'thung ba) Pth. without shame drinking such great quantities of beer; mi-dgé-bai las bág-med-par byéd-pa (mi dge bai las bag med par byed pa) to sin without fear or restraint Dzl.; °dod-č'ags-la bág-med-pas ('dod chags la bag med pas) to indulge in sensuality without restraint Dzl.; heedlessness with regard to good and evil Tar. 4, 22; moral carelessness, indifference, want of principle, bág-med-la nyál-ba (bag med la nyal ba) C., stated to be = bág-la nyal-ba (bag la nyal ba), v. bag (bag) II; of an op-
